Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd kind of wait until I felt like it or until something was due soon. Most weeks I'd maybe put in a couple hours here and there, usually late at night when the house was quiet. I know I should be more organized, but I just couldn't find the motivation to stick to any schedule. Sometimes I'd go days without touching anything course-related.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. If nothing was due, I probably wasn't studying. Some weeks I'd cram everything into one or two days, other weeks I'd barely look at the materials at all. It depended a lot on what else was going on in my life and how I was feeling that day.
Q3: Usually it was because an assignment was coming up or I got a notification that something was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, then end up scrolling through without really doing much. Occasionally a classmate would mention something in the discussion boards and I'd go see what was happening. But I rarely logged in just because I wanted to learn something new.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. There were maybe a few weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit, but it never stuck. The LMS just felt like another thing I had to do rather than something that fit naturally into my day. I'd use it when I had to, but it never became automatic or comfortable for me.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful because I could watch them while doing other things or lying in bed. I'm a hands-on learner, so just reading stuff doesn't work great for me, but videos at least gave me something to watch and follow along with. Some of the discussion posts were helpful too when other students explained things in simpler terms than the lectures did.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the extra readings and optional materials. They just felt like too much effort for something that wasn't going to be graded. The peer interaction stuff I mostly avoided too because I didn't really know anyone in the course and reaching out felt awkward. If it wasn't required, I usually told myself I'd get to it later and then never did.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to an assignment or exam, then it felt worth doing. Activities that seemed practical or that I could apply to something real also felt more worthwhile. But if something was just theoretical or felt like busywork, I'd lose interest pretty quickly. I needed to see the point of it right away or I'd drift off.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely skippable to me. Required activities had this weight to them because I knew they affected my grade, so I'd push through even when I didn't feel like it. But optional things? My brain just categorized them as 'not important' even when they probably would have helped me understand things better. There was no urgency there.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs and then study from those on my tablet without being connected. I also used YouTube a lot to find videos that explained concepts in different ways. Sometimes I'd text friends from other schools who'd taken similar courses and ask them to explain stuff to me. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be working through problems in a notebook or re-reading downloaded notes while the LMS showed nothing. I'd also sometimes study with a friend in person, talking through concepts over coffee. None of that would show up anywhere. The system only sees clicks and logins, not the thinking happening in my head.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or a PDF, watch or read it once online, and then go back to it multiple times offline. My internet at home is fine, but I just preferred having things saved so I could study without distractions from notifications. The LMS would only show that one initial access even though I might have reviewed the material three or four times.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where everything felt harder. I've been dealing with this underlying sadness that makes it tough to care about anything sometimes. When that hit, even simple tasks felt overwhelming. My attendance dropped a lot during those periods, and I'd just watch the deadlines pass feeling paralyzed. The peer support helped a bit, but it didn't fix the root of the problem.
Q13: Absolutely. When I had work shifts or extracurricular commitments piling up, the course was the first thing I'd let slide. Fatigue was a big one too—after a long day, I just couldn't make myself focus on coursework. Confusion also made me avoid things; if I didn't understand a topic, I'd put off dealing with it rather than asking for help, which only made things worse.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through materials but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd watch a video and realize at the end that I hadn't absorbed anything. It was like going through the motions without actually being present. Those sessions felt like a waste even though technically I was 'engaged' according to the system.
Q15: All the time. I'd know something was important but convince myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd push it again. Part of it was anxiety about not doing it well enough, so avoiding it felt safer in the moment. The sadness made it worse because I just didn't have the energy to face things that felt difficult or overwhelming.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd ignore everything until a deadline was close, then panic and try to get it done. The LMS would probably show these spikes of activity right before due dates and almost nothing in between. It's not a healthy way to work, but it's the only way I could get myself to actually do things.
Q17: When I got feedback, it sometimes motivated me to try harder on the next thing, but other times it just made me feel worse about myself. If the feedback was critical, I'd avoid looking at future feedback because I didn't want to feel that way again. Positive feedback helped, but it was rare enough that I couldn't count on it to keep me going.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which sounds good but actually made it harder for me. Without strict schedules, I'd just drift and procrastinate. Having 13 online courses meant everything blurred together and I couldn't keep track of what was due when. A more rigid structure might have forced me to stay on track better.
Q19: The flexibility made engagement harder because I need external structure to function. The discussion requirements were okay because they gave me a reason to interact, but three peer interactions felt awkward and forced. The video content was good for my learning style since I learn better by watching and doing than by reading. But overall, the design assumed more self-discipline than I have.
Q20: I think sometimes you're trying to figure things out or searching for something specific without actually producing work. Or maybe you're anxious and keep checking the LMS without being able to commit to starting an assignment. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes it's just nervous energy or procrastination disguised as engagement.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. My exam scores and assignments were decent even though I wasn't logging in constantly. I did a lot of studying offline with downloaded materials and external resources. The LMS activity doesn't capture studying from notes or talking through concepts with friends. You can learn without constantly clicking around the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on. But they miss everything that happens offline, all the thinking and struggling and re-reading that doesn't involve the platform. They can't measure understanding or emotional engagement. They see behavior but not the quality of that behavior or what's driving it.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting real mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being present and curious. For me, true engagement is rare because of how I've been feeling. I go through the motions a lot without that genuine investment that real engagement requires.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally to even open it some days. It wouldn't capture the sadness that made everything feel pointless or the anxiety that made me avoid things. It also wouldn't show the learning I did outside the platform or the conversations with peers that helped me understand concepts. The data is just the surface of a much messier reality.
